senses.gloss: age (A particular period of time in history)

etymology.text: Borrowed from another pronunciation in Aramaic than زَمَان (zamān).

(Msb, TA;) the first of which [in the order in which they are commonly mentioned by the Arabs, i. e. autumn,] is called by the Arabs [of the classical age]
; called by the former name because the first rain is therein, giving growth to [the herbage called] the
; and called by the latter name because the fruits are gathered therein; and it commences when the sun enters Libra: the second [i. e. winter] is called
; and commences when the sun enters Capricornus: the third [i. e. spring] is
; and commences when the sun enters Aries: the fourth [i. e. summer] is

; and commences when the sun enters Cancer: (Msb:) * * The two following tables exhibit the principal divisions of the Arabian Calendar. The latter of them shows the places of the months in relation to the solar year at the period when they received the names by which they are here designated.

it is also applied to the
period, of the reign, rule, prefecture,
of a man:] with the philosophers, it signifies the
measure of the motion of the ninth

): (TA:) [and there are various other explanations belonging to the conventional language of the schools, not to the proper language of the Arabs: (see the “ Dict. of the Technical Terms used in the Sciences of the Musalmans: ”)] the pl. (of
, see below.] In the following trad.,
When the time becomes contracted, the dream of the believer will scarcely ever,

], what is meant is the end of time; and the approach of the resurrection; because when a thing becomes little, its extremities contract: or what is meant is the day's and the night's becoming equal; for the interpreters of dreams assert that the times [of dreams] most true of interpretation are the season of the breaking forth of the blossoms and that of the ripening of the fruit, which is when the day and the night become equal: or what is meant is the coming forth of El-Mahdee, when the year will be like the month, and the month like the week, and the week like the day, and the day like the hour, deemed short because deemed delightful: (K in art.
:) or it alludes to the shortness of lives and the scantiness of blessings. (TA in that art.) In another trad. it is said,
She used to come to us
I have not met him for a long time
past: but in this case, accord. to the more approved usage, one should say
]. (Lh, K, * TA.) (For authorities, and further information, see the words here mentioned, and more particularly
; under the latter of which it is said that the third and last of the Six Seasons are called by some, respectively,
; and also that the appellations of the 3rd and 4th months are differently pronounced by different persons; and that some exclude the
: and for the Calendar of the Mansions of the Moon, see

.) The months are said to have received the names here given to them from Kiláb Ibn-Murrah, an ancestor of Mohammad, about two centuries before El-Islám. These months were lunar; and from this period, with the view of adapting their year to the solar, the Arabs added a month, which they called

, at the end of every three years, until they were forbidden to do so by the Kur-án (ch. ix.): but the months still retrograded through the seasons, though much more slowly. The abolition of the intercalation was proclaimed by Mohammad at the pilgrimage in the tenth year of the Flight.
